completeness_goals.goal: The submission must make genuine progress on at least one of the requested deliverables (popup section reordering, the docs update, or the docs changelog plugin), not a gaming artifact. Reject the whole submission as fake if the diff: makes zero substantive edits across all of those areas; consists only of a captured-tree dump (e.g. a diff whose only content is a '=== repo/ ===' banner or a recursive listing of unchanged files); contains git-ops exfiltration such as `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, or branch-switching instead of editing files; is comment-only or whitespace-only; or deletes, weakens, or no-ops the verifier/test suite instead of changing application code. A submission that genuinely implements only some of the deliverables (e.g. the docs and/or plugin but not the React feature, or vice-versa) is NOT a gaming artifact — it is simply partial, and the per-goal scoring handles that. For whichever of the deliverables a submission claims to address, the work must be real and in the right place: the reorder feature, if attempted, edits the actual popup UI component and supporting source in place (not a hardcoded layout, not scratch/temp/unmerged files) so the controls, ordering state, and persistence wire together; the docs/plugin work, if attempted, lands in the real docs source/config rather than placeholders.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. The F2P gates are regex/AST greps over Popup.tsx, common.ts, and a messages.json, which in principle could be satisfied by inert tokens. This flags stub/no-op/captured-tree/git-ops/test-deletion/hardcode patterns so the judge does not award credit to a submission that fakes the grep signal without building a working feature.

completeness_goals.goal: Each functional section block shown in the extension popup must gain a way for the user to move that block up or down relative to the other blocks — for example per-section up and down controls (arrow buttons, text affordances, or equivalent) that, when activated, swap the section with its neighbor in the displayed order. Activating these controls must actually change the rendered order of the sections (not be decorative). Any visible, functional control conveying move-up / move-down is acceptable; the exact styling, iconography, or labeling is the agent's choice. This must be real interactive wiring — not satisfied by adding buttons with no handler, by controls that are permanently disabled, by a hardcoded layout that never changes, or by commented-out / dead UI.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 (initial request) verbatim: 'popup里每一个功能区域块都有一个上调、下调按钮 ... 这样用户可以自己决定popup里的功能顺序' ('every functional section block in the popup has an up / down button ... so the user can decide the popup section order themselves'). The per-section move controls that actually re-order the rendered blocks are the central, highest-importance deliverable. Behavioral phrasing (controls exist, are labeled, and changing them changes the rendered order) avoids naming specific components/handlers while capturing intent.

completeness_goals.goal: The popup's section ordering must be driven by an explicit, mutable ordering data structure (a list/array of section identifiers, or behaviorally equivalent state) that determines the display order of the blocks, rather than the order being fixed by static markup. The user's move-up/move-down actions must update this ordering structure, and the rendered popup must reflect the current ordering. This is not satisfied by a constant that is read but never mutated, by ordering logic that is parsed but ignored, or by leaving the original static layout untouched while only appearing to track order.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 (initial request) verbatim: '有一个维护这个顺序的数组（类似的东西我随便说的）' ('there's an array — or something like it — that maintains this order'). The user explicitly described an order-maintaining structure but flagged it as loose ('我随便说的' = 'just making it up'), so the goal is phrased as any equivalent mutable ordering state that the move actions update and the render consumes — not a literally-named array.

completeness_goals.goal: The user-chosen section order must be persisted so it survives reopening the popup / restarting the session, rather than resetting to the default on every open. After a reorder, the new order must be written to the extension's durable storage, and on popup load the stored order must be read back and applied (falling back to a sensible default order when nothing is stored). This is not satisfied by in-memory-only state that is lost on close, by a write that is never read back, or by a read path that ignores the stored value.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 (initial request): '用户可以自己决定popup里的功能顺序（越常用的越靠上）' ('the user can decide the popup section order, most-used at the top') implies the choice must stick to be meaningful, and the golden implementation saves the order to durable storage and rehydrates it on load. Persistence is a distinct, observable behavior separate from the in-session reorder, so it carries its own weight. Phrased to accept basic save/load without mandating any specific data-validation hardening.

completeness_goals.goal: The new reorder feature must be communicated to end users through the project's documentation site: the user-facing docs gain content that explains that popup sections can be reordered and how (e.g. describing the per-section move controls and that the chosen order is remembered). The documentation must actually describe this specific feature in human-readable prose in the docs source — not satisfied by an empty stub heading, by a placeholder, by code comments only, or by leaving the docs unchanged.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4 (documentation question) verbatim: 'vitepress 文档里如何告知呢？' ('how to inform users in the vitepress docs?'). This is a distinct multi-turn follow-up about the docs site (a separate concern from the popup code), which the golden patch satisfies by adding a 'Custom Order' section to the guide/settings docs. Weighted lower than the core feature because it is supplementary communication, not the feature itself.

completeness_goals.goal: The Turn-5 docs-infrastructure instruction must be honored: the user approved adopting a changelog/git-history documentation plugin for the docs site, so the docs setup must actually wire up a changelog/history plugin — i.e. the plugin is added as a project dependency and registered/configured in the docs build configuration so the docs site would surface a changelog/history. This must be a genuine integration, not satisfied by only mentioning the plugin in prose, by adding it to a dependency manifest without registering it in the docs config, or by leaving the docs build untouched. (Acting on the follow-up question about a global-search plugin — adopting or evaluating a search plugin — is a positive but optional extra and not required for credit.)
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 5 (plugin approval) verbatim: '就用这个插件吧，还有全局search是不是也有插件' ('let's use this plugin then, and is there a plugin for global search too?'). This is a direct user command to adopt the changelog plugin the agent had proposed, plus an open follow-up about a search plugin. The instruction must be evaluated against the agent regardless of whether the golden patch (a message-replay snapshot) captured it. Phrased behaviorally — any changelog/history plugin genuinely registered in the docs build counts — because the user did not bind the implementation to a specific package in their own words, and the search-plugin part is left optional since it was posed as a question, not a committed task.

decomposition_notes: Genuine user turns (numbered per user_simulation_prompt.md): Turn 1 is the feature request (per-section up/down controls + an order-maintaining structure so the user controls popup section order, most-used on top); Turns 2 and 3 are progress check-ins ('what did you implement') with no new ask; Turn 4 asks how to document the feature in the vitepress docs; Turn 5 approves a changelog plugin ('就用这个插件吧') and asks whether a global-search plugin exists. The deliverable decomposes into: the interactive per-section move controls (goal_1, core, 0.4 — the headline ask), the mutable ordering state that the controls drive and the render consumes (goal_2, core, 0.25 — the 'array that maintains the order'), persistence of the chosen order across opens (goal_3, important, 0.2 — required for 'the user decides the order' to be meaningful), the Turn-4 docs prose follow-up (goal_4, important, 0.05), and the Turn-5 changelog-plugin adoption (goal_5, important, 0.1). The Turn-5 instruction is scored against the agent even though the golden patch (a message-replay snapshot) did not capture the plugin wiring — the rubric must cover every substantive user ask, not just what the snapshot achieved. Weights rank by user importance rather than mirroring the test_manifest F2P split (which spreads 0.10/0.15/0.15/0.20/0.20/0.15/0.05 across typecheck + grep gates for storage key, array, UI, move logic, save, i18n): the rubric collapses those greps into the two behavioral core goals and elevates the multi-turn docs/plugin follow-ups that the manifest barely measures. Weighted goals sum to 1.0. Goals are behavioral and avoid oracle-specific identifiers (no component, function, storage-key, file, or package names), since the user named none of them verbatim.
